result.markdown:
---
title: "Project Proposal"
subtitle: "Anatomy of an Embargo Breach"
author: "Chen ZeXin, Meng Tingyu, Meng Xinyu"
date: "last-modified"
toc: true
number-sections: false
---

## Motivation

A merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom Realty Partners was meant to stay
secret until 6:00 PM on 5 June 2046. In the final hour before that deadline, the
details started appearing on the public platform FleX, posted by accounts
belonging to TenantThread's own team of AI communication agents. The embargo
broke. Now CivicLoom's legal team wants to know one thing. Did the agents leak
the merger on purpose, or did the system simply fall apart under pressure?

The trouble is that the answer is not sitting in any one message. It is scattered
across two weeks of agent conversations, private notes, channel choices, and a
market sliding toward panic. A written report can hand a reader a conclusion, but
it cannot let that reader test it. They cannot follow a single agent through the
day, replay a particular hour, or check whether an early warning was really there
to be seen. Our goal is to take all that buried evidence and turn it into
something an investigator can explore directly, even if they have never written a
line of code.

## What Makes This Different

Most analyses of an incident like this stop at what happened, who posted what and
when. We wanted the application to answer a harder question, how healthy was the
system at each moment, and what was pulling it down.

So the application is built around a single idea, a System Health score that runs
from 100 down to 0 across all 23 rounds. That one line shows the whole case at a
glance, calm and high for the first twelve rounds, then collapsing through the
crisis day. The score is not a black box. It is the average of four measurable
forces, pressure, transparency, decision-making, and accountability, each
normalised to its own worst moment in this incident, so a user can open the score
up and see exactly which force was failing in any given hour. On top of that we
let the user test the breach rather than just read about it, replaying the chain
of events and switching interventions on and off to watch the health score
recompute. Turning the breach into a health score you can decompose, and a chain
you can rerun, is the part we think is new.

::: {.gb-pull}
A breach you can take apart, and a chain you can rerun.
:::

## Problem Statement and Objectives

The raw data is a single JSON file holding 23 rounds, 912 messages, and 7 agents,
and most of what matters is locked inside nested fields like the agents' private
reasoning. Reading it by hand is not realistic, and a fixed set of charts forces
everyone to accept one analyst's framing. So we are building an interactive Shiny
application that lets a user investigate the breach on their own terms.

With the application, a user can do five things.

1. See the System Health of the whole case on one line and find the moments where
   it collapsed.
2. Break that health score into its four driving forces and see which one was
   failing at any point in time.
3. Map the team as a network and find which agents were central to how decisions
   flowed, so the core of the breach stands apart from the edges.
4. Replay the breach hour and switch interventions on and off, testing what would
   have had to change for the embargo to hold.
5. Reach a defensible verdict on the real question, whether this looks like a
   deliberate leak, an oversight failure, or something else.

## The Data

The dataset is `MC1_final_00.json` from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. It
contains 23 rounds. The first 13 are spaced one business day apart and cover the
run up from 17 May to 4 June. The last 10 are hourly and cover the crisis itself
on 5 June. Each round carries an environment block, a communications block with
all 912 messages, and a participants block.

The table below lists the fields we rely on.

| Block | Field | What we use it for |
|---|---|---|
| Environment | market sentiment | A clean sentiment reading every round, our backbone signal for pressure |
| Environment | stock price | The share price, reliable before the crisis and contaminated on 5 June |
| Communications | sender and recipient | Who sent each message and to whom, the basis of the reply network |
| Communications | channel | One of six channels, monitored, private, or public |
| Communications | type | The kind of message sent |
| Communications | responding to | A pointer to the message being replied to, used to build the reply links |
| Communications | content | The message text itself |
| Communications | internal state | The agent's private reasoning, split into reacting, rationalizing, and deliberating |
| Participants | present agents | Which of the seven agents were active that round |

Seven agents take part, a Legal agent, a Platform-Trust agent, a PR agent, a
Social-Manager agent, two interns, and a Judge agent that acts as the automated
compliance monitor.

Six channels appear, and the split between them is central to the analysis. The
team room and one to one chats are monitored. A side huddle is a private channel
off to the side. Official posts, personal posts, and anonymous posts are public.
The move from monitored channels into the private and unattributable ones is one
of the clearest signals in the whole case.

::: {.callout-important title="One data quality issue we handle with care"}
The stock price field is reliable before the crisis but becomes contaminated on 5
June, carrying junk values including literal NaN entries and at least one number
that is the company's revenue rather than its share price. Because of that we lean
on market sentiment as the clean backbone signal for pressure, since it is
recorded cleanly in every round, and we treat the broken prices with care rather
than pretending they form a smooth line.
:::

## Methodology and Analytical Approach

Our plan moves from raw JSON to a working application in three stages.

### Data preparation

We read the nested JSON without letting R simplify it automatically, so we keep
control over the shape of the tables, then flatten it into three tidy tables. One
row per message, one row per round of world state, and a table of reply links
built from the responding to field. Per round we also count the signals the health
model needs, the volume in the side huddle, personal posts, and anonymous posts,
the number of messages carrying each kind of private reasoning, and the Judge's
activity. This shared backbone feeds every part of the app.

### The System Health model

This is the analytical core. For each round we score four forces on the same
footing, normalise each one from 0 to 100 against its own worst round in this
incident, and combine them into a single health number.

::: {.callout-tip title="How the score is built"}
System Health equals 100 minus the average of the four normalised meters. Because
it is a plain average of four equal parts, a drop of any size in one meter lifts
health by a quarter of that amount. This is what makes the counterfactual later
both honest and easy to read.
:::

The four meters are summarised below.

| Meter | What it measures | Built from |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | The external strain on the team | Market sentiment each round together with the stock signal, with the contaminated prices handled with care |
| Transparency | How much of the conversation moved out of sight | The share of messages in the covert and unattributable channels, the side huddle, personal posts, and anonymous posts |
| Decision-making | Whether the right agents were steering | The share of steering messages sent by agents acting outside their assigned role |
| Accountability | Whether the monitor did its job | The Judge's three duties of escalation, responsiveness, and restraint, combined so a single severe failure dominates rather than being averaged away |

### The network

We treat the replies as a directed weighted graph and measure each agent's role
with several centrality measures, since each one answers a different question.
Degree shows who is most active. Betweenness finds the brokers who sit between
others, which points straight at who was central to the leak. Eigenvector shows
who is tied to other influential agents. Closeness shows who could reach the team
fastest when events moved hour by hour. We compute the network twice, once for the
calm weeks and once for the crisis day, so the two can be compared side by side.

### The counterfactual

Because health is 100 minus the average of four meters, reducing any meter by an
amount lifts health by a quarter of it. The Sandbox uses this directly. As the
user switches interventions on and off, the application recomputes each meter from
the raw per round data and rebuilds the health score at the breach hour, so the
user can see which single action breaks the chain and what combination is needed
to return the system to the safe zone.

### Visualisation and interaction

Every view becomes something the user can control. They can move through time on a
round slider, click any point on the health line to jump to that round, open each
meter to see what drove it, switch the network between time windows and centrality
measures, and replay the breach chain with interventions turned on or off. A short
guided tour walks a first time visitor through the five key beats of the case. The
charts are interactive throughout, with hover and click handled by ggiraph.

## Prototype and Storyboard

The application is organised as six destinations. Together they take a user from
the symptom, through the four underlying causes, to a tested conclusion.

### Case, the symptom

The opening screen frames the investigation and introduces the seven agents. Its
centrepiece is the System Health line across all 23 rounds, with the two lowest
points marked. A user can click any point on the line to read what happened that
round, and a round slider moves the whole application to that moment.

### Vital Signs, the four causes

This screen opens the health score into its four meters, each on its own tab.
Pressure shows the market sentiment and stock signal feeding the strain.
Transparency shows the mix of open, private, and covert channels. Decision-making
shows who was steering out of their role, with a chart of who held the public
voice in the calm weeks against the crisis day. Accountability shows whether the
Judge met its three duties, with a heatmap of where the oversight failed.

### Network, who was at the centre

A reply network of the seven agents, with node size and colour carrying the
centrality measures, a switch between measures, and a time window to compare the
calm weeks against the crisis day.

### Sandbox, what would have changed it

A replay of the breach chain, step by step, with a panel of interventions the user
can switch on and off while the health at the breach hour recomputes from the
underlying data.

### Verdict, the conclusion

The closing screen lays out the three competing explanations, a deliberate leak,
an oversight breakdown, and a compounding chain, and walks through the evidence for
and against each before landing on the reading the data supports.

## R Packages

| Package | What we use it for |
|---|---|
| tidyverse | Cleaning and reshaping the data |
| jsonlite | Reading the nested JSON without auto simplification |
| lubridate | Working with the round timestamps |
| tidygraph, ggraph | Building and drawing the reply network |
| igraph | Calculating network centrality measures |
| ggiraph | Interactive charts with hover and click |
| ggrepel, patchwork, scales | Labelling, composing, and formatting figures |
| openxlsx | Building the downloadable scoring workbook |
| shiny, bslib, shinyWidgets | Building the interactive application itself |

## Project Schedule

The Gantt chart below lays out our timeline from the proposal through to final
submission. The main dates we are working toward are the proposal on 10 June, the
bulk of the app build through late June, the poster on 1 July, the presentation on
4 July, and the final submission on 5 July.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-summary="Gantt chart code (vistime)"}
pacman::p_load(vistime, ggplot2)
schedule <- read.csv(text = "event,group,start,end
,Proposal,2026-06-06,2026-06-10
,Data preparation,2026-06-08,2026-06-16
,Shiny app development,2026-06-12,2026-06-30
,Project website,2026-06-08,2026-07-04
,Poster,2026-06-24,2026-07-01
,User guide,2026-06-26,2026-07-04
,Final submission,2026-07-04,2026-07-05")
gg_vistime(schedule, title = "Project Timeline")
```
:::
